trajectory_summary: The call moves cleanly from a slightly confused opening to a booked Saturday appointment with minimal friction. Karen is a cooperative, motivated customer who drove much of the call's forward momentum herself. The rep completed the structural beats adequately but left two bill-flip opportunities on the table and missed a cross-sell signal on the detached garage. The appointment is real but the closer walks in without the emotional groundwork the setter could have laid.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is a booked appointment for Saturday at 09:30 with both Karen and Eric available. The appointment has one structural vulnerability: the confirmation email was directed to Karen's address (klynspanbauer@gmail.com) after the rep initially had eric@gmail.com on file — if the confirmation goes to the wrong address, Karen may not receive it. The booking itself is genuine; the no-show risk is logistical, not motivational.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: That's about $380 a month with a lot. Oh, wow. Why it's so high.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Karen handed the rep bill pain twice (06:42 at $380, 17:30 at $700 peak) and both times the rep acknowledged the number and moved on. The schema flags this as the universal floor-wide gap. On this call it's especially visible because the $700 disclosure came after the appointment was already booked — there was zero scheduling risk in pausing to flip it. The rep has the instinct to acknowledge; the missing move is turning the customer's own number back into the reason to show on Saturday. This is the single highest-leverage coaching beat for this rep.

key_moments.quote: That's about $380 a month with a lot. Oh, wow. Why it's so high.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen volunteers $380/month and immediately expresses her own confusion about why it's so high — this is the emotional opening for a flip. The rep responds 'Yeah. That's super high. I could definitely see where you guys are, looking into this.' — a warm acknowledgment that captures the number and drops it. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; the rep executed 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' Strategic read: rep was mid-verify and may have been holding the flip for later. Reactive read: this is the floor-wide habit the schema flags — bill captured, emotional alignment-against-utility never built. The $700 disclosure at 17:30 confirms the reactive read; the rep had a second chance and repeated the same pattern.

key_moments.quote: The last not last month, but month before, three months, it's, like, $700 a month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen volunteers peak bill pain — $700/month for three consecutive months — after the appointment is already booked. The rep responds 'It was that high?' and then immediately pivots to close-out ('Sounds good. Awesome. Well, super excited to get this out to you, Karen.'). This is the second instance of the same capture-and-drop pattern. The appointment is booked, so there is no scheduling risk in pausing here — the rep had a clean window to anchor this number as the reason Karen should show on Saturday. The consistent pattern across both instances confirms reactive habit, not strategic restraint.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We're looking for solar panels for our home and garage. I've got to detach. I have no electricity. So I'm thinking about solar panels on the garage and on the house.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen volunteers a non-standard installation scenario: a detached garage built in 2025 with no existing electrical connection, where she wants solar to serve as the primary power source. This is a meaningful qualification signal — the customer is thinking about a dual-structure install, not just a standard rooftop system. The rep acknowledges it ('Okay. Sounds good. Awesome.') and moves on without anchoring the complexity or flagging it for the closer. The garage scenario is not a standard solar install and may require a different scoping conversation at the closer stage.

key_moments.quote: And let you know that my husband and I are deaf. I'm talking through a relay service. So we we could communicate. Use, Google Translate to to understand each other.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen discloses mid-close that both she and Eric are deaf and have been communicating through a relay service the entire call. The rep responds: 'Okay. That works. I'll go ahead and put that down for the notes here. So I appreciate you for letting me know.' The acknowledgment is adequate — the rep doesn't stumble or dismiss it. But the disclosure has direct implications for the Saturday appointment that the rep does not address: the closer needs to know the communication channel in advance, not discover it at the start of the appointment. The rep's promise to 'put that down for the notes' is the right move; the coaching gap is whether those notes actually reach the closer.
